name: Algonquin Provincial Park
description: Ontario's most famous park, Algonquin is a vast wilderness of maple hills, rocky ridges, and over 2,400 lakes. Known for incredible canoeing, wildlife viewing including moose and wolves, and stunning fall colors. This iconic park offers both backcountry adventure and accessible camping.

images.altText: Canoe on a pristine lake in Algonquin Provincial Park
Fall colors reflecting on water in Algonquin

name: Killarney Provincial Park
description: Famous for its stunning white quartzite ridges, crystal-clear lakes, and pink granite shores immortalized by the Group of Seven painters. The La Cloche Mountains offer spectacular hiking with panoramic views. A paddler's paradise with some of Ontario's clearest waters.

images.altText: White quartzite ridges of the La Cloche Mountains
Crystal clear waters of George Lake, Killarney

name: Sandbanks Provincial Park
description: Home to the world's largest freshwater baymouth barrier dune formation and massive sand dunes. The park features beautiful sandy beaches on Lake Ontario, perfect for swimming and sunbathing. Popular with families for its warm, shallow waters and picturesque West Lake.

images.altText: Sandy beaches and dunes at Sandbanks Provincial Park
Sunset over West Lake at Sandbanks

name: Presqu'ile Provincial Park
description: A limestone peninsula jutting into Lake Ontario, renowned as one of Ontario's premier bird watching destinations with over 330 species recorded. Features sandy beaches, a historic lighthouse, and important wetland habitats. Excellent for swimming and nature interpretation programs.

images.altText: Historic lighthouse at Presqu'ile Provincial Park
Bird watchers on the marsh boardwalk

name: Bon Echo Provincial Park
description: Dominated by the massive Mazinaw Rock, a 100-meter-high cliff face rising from Mazinaw Lake featuring over 260 Indigenous pictographs. This dramatic landscape offers excellent rock climbing, deep-water paddling, and cultural heritage experiences. Named after the remarkable echo that bounces off the cliff.

images.altText: Mazinaw Rock rising from the lake at Bon Echo
Indigenous pictographs on the cliff face

name: Arrowhead Provincial Park
description: A four-season destination known for its spectacular fall colors and winter activities. Features excellent cross-country skiing trails and a popular skating trail on Arrowhead Lake. In summer, enjoy swimming, hiking, and exploring the scenic Stubb's Falls along the park's nature trails.

images.altText: Ice skating trail on Arrowhead Lake in winter
Stubb's Falls surrounded by autumn colors

name: Pinery Provincial Park
description: Protects one of the last remaining oak savanna and dune ecosystems in Ontario along Lake Huron's southeastern shore. Miles of sandy beaches, rare Karner Blue butterfly habitat, and diverse ecosystems. Excellent for swimming, birding, and learning about unique Ontario ecology.

images.altText: Sandy beach along Lake Huron at Pinery
Oak savanna ecosystem with hiking trail

name: Grundy Lake Provincial Park
description: A hidden gem offering classic Canadian Shield landscape with rocky outcrops, forests, and pristine lakes. Less crowded than nearby parks, it's perfect for peaceful canoeing and backcountry camping. The park provides excellent access to wildlife viewing and northern wilderness experiences.

images.altText: Rocky Canadian Shield shoreline at Grundy Lake
Peaceful canoeing on Grundy Lake

name: Lake Superior Provincial Park
description: A rugged wilderness park along the spectacular northeastern shore of Lake Superior. Features dramatic coastal cliffs, pristine rivers, and the famous Group of Seven landscapes. Offers both roadside camping and extensive backcountry trails through boreal forest and along rocky shorelines.

images.altText: Coastal cliffs along Lake Superior shoreline
Sunset over Lake Superior from the park

name: Killbear Provincial Park
description: Located on Georgian Bay's stunning eastern shore, Killbear features pink granite rocks, windswept pines, and crystal-clear waters. The park's unique landscape and incredible sunsets make it a photographer's dream. Popular for family camping with excellent swimming beaches and paddling opportunities.

images.altText: Pink granite rocks and Georgian Bay waters at Killbear
Sunset through windswept pines at Killbear

name: Frontenac Provincial Park
description: A backcountry canoe and hiking park protecting ancient Frontenac Arch landscape connecting the Canadian Shield to the Adirondacks. Over 160 backcountry campsites accessible by canoe or on foot. Known for excellent wildlife viewing including black bears, loons, and diverse bird species.

images.altText: Backcountry lake and campsite in Frontenac
Hiking trail through Frontenac Arch landscape

name: Silent Lake Provincial Park
description: A peaceful wilderness park perfect for paddlers and hikers seeking tranquility. The park's crown jewel is Silent Lake itself, surrounded by hardwood forests with excellent fall colors. No motorboats allowed, ensuring a serene wilderness experience for canoers and kayakers.

images.altText: Peaceful morning mist on Silent Lake
Canoe gliding across calm Silent Lake waters

name: Petroglyphs Provincial Park
description: Home to the largest concentration of Indigenous rock carvings in Canada, with over 900 petroglyphs carved into marble bedrock. This sacred site offers cultural education and spiritual significance. The Teaching Rocks are protected under a modern pavilion while maintaining their sacred atmosphere.

images.altText: Indigenous petroglyphs at the Teaching Rocks
Educational pavilion protecting the ancient carvings

name: Quetico Provincial Park
description: One of North America's premier wilderness canoe parks, protecting vast boreal forests and pristine waterways along the Minnesota border. Famous for world-class boundary waters canoeing, fishing, and complete wilderness immersion. Over 2,000 campsites accessible only by canoe.

images.altText: Wilderness canoe route in Quetico Provincial Park
Backcountry campsite on a pristine Quetico lake

name: Sleeping Giant Provincial Park
description: Featuring the iconic Sleeping Giant formation - a massive cliff resembling a reclining giant - on the Sibley Peninsula. Offers stunning views of Lake Superior, challenging hiking including the famous Top of the Giant trail. Rich in Indigenous legends and natural beauty.

images.altText: The Sleeping Giant formation viewed from Lake Superior
Panoramic view from the Top of the Giant trail

name: Awenda Provincial Park
description: Located on Georgian Bay's Penetanguishene Peninsula, featuring beautiful sandy beaches, coastal dunes, and diverse forests. The park offers exceptional bird watching, particularly during migration seasons. Family-friendly with great swimming and accessible hiking trails through unique ecosystems.

images.altText: Sandy beach on Georgian Bay at Awenda
Forest trail through diverse ecosystems

name: Algonquin Highlands
description: A constellation of smaller parks including Oxtongue River-Ragged Falls showcasing powerful waterfalls and classic Muskoka landscape. Features beautiful hiking trails to scenic waterfalls and pristine river environments. Perfect for day trips and nature photography.

images.altText: Ragged Falls cascading through the forest
Hiking trail along the Oxtongue River

name: Rushing River Provincial Park
description: Named for the rushing waterways flowing through the park, offering excellent opportunities for canoeing and fishing. Features beautiful forest trails, sandy beaches, and family-friendly camping. The park's rivers and lakes are perfect for paddling adventures.

images.altText: Rapids on the rushing river
Sandy beach and calm waters for swimming

name: MacGregor Point Provincial Park
description: A Lake Huron shoreline park known for its incredible dark skies and astronomy programs. Features sandy beaches, diverse trails through coastal forests and wetlands, and excellent bird watching. The park is a designated Dark Sky Preserve offering spectacular stargazing.

images.altText: Starry night sky at MacGregor Point Dark Sky Preserve
Lake Huron beach at sunset

name: Charleston Lake Provincial Park
description: A beautiful park featuring clear blue-green waters perfect for swimming and boating. The rugged Canadian Shield landscape meets southern hardwood forests creating unique biodiversity. Excellent hiking trails offer scenic lookouts over the lake and surrounding wilderness.

images.altText: Clear blue-green waters of Charleston Lake
Scenic lookout over Charleston Lake

name: Kawartha Highlands Provincial Park
description: Ontario's second-largest park offering extensive backcountry wilderness experiences. Features hundreds of pristine lakes connected by canoe routes and hiking trails. Perfect for multi-day paddling trips and wilderness camping in a landscape rich with Canadian Shield beauty.

images.altText: Wilderness lake in Kawartha Highlands
Backcountry canoe route through pristine landscape

name: Missisagi Provincial Park
description: A wilderness park featuring the scenic Mississagi River and beautiful Semiwite Lake. Offers remote camping experiences with excellent canoeing and kayaking opportunities. The park protects important boreal forest habitat and provides true northern Ontario wilderness adventure.

images.altText: Mississagi River flowing through the park
Semiwite Lake at dawn

name: Balsam Lake Provincial Park
description: Located in the heart of the Kawarthas on Ontario's highest lake, offering excellent fishing and boating. Features beautiful sandy beaches and family-friendly camping. The park's elevation provides cooler summer temperatures and stunning panoramic views of the surrounding landscape.

images.altText: Sandy beach on Balsam Lake
Sunset over Ontario's highest lake

name: Rondeau Provincial Park
description: A Carolinian forest haven on Lake Erie featuring rare plant and animal species found nowhere else in Canada. One of Ontario's premier bird watching destinations with over 330 species recorded. Beautiful beaches, unique ecosystem diversity, and important monarch butterfly migration stopover.

images.altText: Rare Carolinian forest in Rondeau
Lake Erie beach at Rondeau Provincial Park

name: Obabika River Provincial Park
description: A pristine wilderness waterway park protecting the Obabika River system and surrounding forests. Perfect for backcountry canoe trips with numerous portages and remote campsites. The park offers true wilderness solitude and excellent fishing in crystal-clear waters.

images.altText: Obabika River winding through wilderness
Remote backcountry campsite on the Obabika

name: Sharbot Lake Provincial Park
description: A smaller park offering peaceful camping on the shores of Sharbot Lake. Features excellent swimming, boating, and fishing opportunities. The park provides a quiet retreat with beautiful sunset views and easy access to nearby communities for supplies.

images.altText: Peaceful waters of Sharbot Lake at sunset
Family camping area near the lake

name: Neys Provincial Park
description: Located on Lake Superior's rugged north shore, featuring dramatic coastal scenery and pristine wilderness beaches. The park is home to a unique woodland caribou herd. Offers excellent hiking along the superior shoreline with stunning views and opportunities to spot caribou.

images.altText: Woodland caribou on Lake Superior shore
Dramatic coastal cliffs at Neys

name: Bronte Creek Provincial Park
description: A unique park near Toronto featuring both natural and agricultural heritage with a working heritage farm. Great trails for hiking and mountain biking through ravines and meadows. Popular for family activities including a large pool and play barn, plus excellent cross-country skiing in winter.

images.altText: Heritage farm at Bronte Creek Provincial Park
Trails through ravines and forest

name: Halfway Lake Provincial Park
description: A peaceful retreat featuring a beautiful lake surrounded by boreal forest. Excellent for family camping with great swimming beaches and calm waters perfect for canoeing. The park offers well-maintained trails and a serene atmosphere away from crowds.

images.altText: Calm waters of Halfway Lake for swimming
Forest trail around Halfway Lake

name: Wasaga Beach Provincial Park
description: Home to the world's longest freshwater beach stretching 14 kilometers along Georgian Bay's Nottawasaga Bay. Features warm, shallow waters perfect for families and beautiful sandy shores. Popular destination for beach activities, water sports, and summer recreation.

images.altText: World's longest freshwater beach at Wasaga
Families enjoying the warm shallow waters

name: Earl Rowe Provincial Park
description: A family-oriented park located near Toronto featuring a large reservoir for swimming and boating. Offers diverse recreational opportunities including disc golf, extensive trails, and a popular beach. Well-maintained campground makes it perfect for weekend getaways from the city.

images.altText: Reservoir beach at Earl Rowe Provincial Park
Disc golf course through forest trails
